verses.commentary: Jericho — Jesus entering. The last stop before Jerusalem (15 miles uphill).
A man named Zacchaeus (Zakchaios, Ζακχαῖος — Hebrew Zakkai, 'pure'). Chief tax collector (architelones, ἀρχιτελώνης — only here in NT). Rich. Pure-named but compromised by profession.
Sought to see who Jesus was, could not on account of the crowd — he was small in stature. Physical disadvantage.
Ran ahead, climbed a sycamore-fig (sykomorea, συκομορέα) tree to see him — for Jesus was about to pass. Adult dignity sacrificed for a glimpse.
Jesus came to the place, looked up: 'Zacchaeus, hurry, come down — today (semeron, σήμερον — Lukan today again) I must (dei, δεῖ — necessary) stay at your house.' Divine necessity in hospitality.
Came down quickly, received him joyfully. Reception is rapid, joyful, total.
All who saw it grumbled: 'He has gone to be the guest of a sinner.' Familiar refrain — Pharisees' complaint now generalized to 'all.'

Zacchaeus stood: 'Behold, Lord — half my goods I give to the poor; if I have defrauded anyone of anything, I restore it fourfold.' Half-giving exceeds requirement; fourfold restitution exceeds the rabbinic standard (Exodus 22:1 — fourfold for stolen sheep).

Jesus: 'Today (semeron, σήμερον) salvation has come to this house — since he also is a son of Abraham.' Salvation declared. Abrahamic-sonship reaffirmed for the chief tax collector — a status the religious would have stripped from him.
'For the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost.' Mission-summary verse. Lukan high-water mark of Jesus' purpose.
As they were listening — he told a parable, because he was near Jerusalem and they supposed the kingdom of God would appear immediately. Correcting expectations.
Parable: a nobleman went into a far country to receive for himself a kingdom and return. (Echo of Herod Archelaus traveling to Rome for confirmation.) The far-and-return shape.
Called ten servants, gave them ten minas (mina = about three months' wages). 'Do business until I come.' Differential entrustment.
His citizens hated him, sent a delegation: 'We do not want this man to reign over us.' (Mirroring Archelaus's actual history — the Jews protested his appointment.)
Returned having received the kingdom — ordered the servants summoned. Reckoning time.
First: 'Lord, your mina has made ten minas more.' Tenfold return.
'Well done, good servant! Because you have been faithful in a very little, you shall have authority over ten cities.' Reward scales beyond ratio.
Second: 'Your mina has made five minas.' Half the first's yield.
'You are to be over five cities.' Proportional reward.
Another: 'Lord, here is your mina — I kept it laid away in a handkerchief.' Preserved, unproductive.
'I was afraid of you, because you are a severe man — you take what you did not deposit, you reap what you did not sow.' Theology of fear stated.
'I will condemn you with your own words, you wicked servant! You knew that I was a severe man —' Grants the premise, indicts the conclusion.
'Why did you not put my money in the bank, that at my coming I might have collected it with interest?' Minimum effort would have sufficed.
Bystanders: take the mina from him, give to the one who has ten minas.
Bystanders: 'Lord, he has ten minas!' Surprised at the redistribution.
'To everyone who has, more will be given; from the one who has not, even what he has will be taken away.' Compounding principle.

'But as for these enemies of mine who did not want me to reign over them — bring them here and slaughter them before me.' Lukan parable has the political subplot Matthew omitted. Eschatological judgment of unwilling subjects.
After saying this — he went on ahead, going up to Jerusalem. Resumed motion toward the destination.
Approaching Bethphage and Bethany at the Mount of Olives — sent two disciples.
'Go into the village ahead. As you enter, you will find a colt tied, on which no one has ever sat. Untie it, bring it.' Detail of unridden colt — ritually pure mount (compare Numbers 19:2).
If anyone asks why — 'The Lord has need of it.' Authoritative formula.
Those sent went and found it just as he had told them. Compliance.
Untying the colt — the owners said: 'Why are you untying it?'
'The Lord has need of it.' The formula works.
Brought it to Jesus — threw their cloaks on the colt — set Jesus on it. Royal-mount preparations improvised.
As he rode along — they spread their cloaks on the road. Royal welcome.
Descending the Mount of Olives — the whole multitude of disciples began to rejoice and praise God with a loud voice for all the mighty works they had seen.
'Blessed is the King who comes in the name of the Lord! Peace in heaven and glory in the highest!' Adapted Psalm 118:26 + heavenly Gloria echo (2:14).
Some Pharisees in the crowd: 'Teacher, rebuke your disciples.' Try to silence the acclamation.
'I tell you, if these were silent, the very stones would cry out.' Habakkuk 2:11 echo. The praise is inevitable; if humans refuse it, creation will testify.
Drew near, saw the city, wept (eklausen, ἔκλαυσεν) over it. Lukan unique scene — Messiah lamenting.
'Would that you, even you, had known on this day the things that make for peace! Now they are hidden from your eyes.' Day-of-visitation lost. The 'today' word is now sorrowful.
Days will come — your enemies will set up a barricade against you, surround you, hem you in on every side. AD 70 siege foretold in operational detail.

Tear you down to the ground — you and your children — they will not leave one stone upon another in you, because you did not know the time of your visitation (episkopes, ἐπισκοπῆς). Visitation language — covenant-keyword (compare 1:68, 78).
Entered the temple, began to drive out those who sold. Lukan version is compressed.
'My house shall be a house of prayer — you have made it a den of robbers.' Isaiah 56:7 + Jeremiah 7:11.
Teaching daily in the temple. Chief priests, scribes, principal men of the people sought to destroy him.
Could not find what to do — all the people hung on him (exekremato, ἐξεκρέματο — were glued) to hear him. Popular protection. The arrest must wait for an unguarded moment.
